splits.legalQuestion: Whether a multiemployer pension plan actuary may select actuarial assumptions for calculating withdrawal liability after the statutory measurement date (the end of the prior plan year), or whether all such assumptions must be fixed by that date.
splits.description: Circuits disagree on whether ERISA requires actuarial assumptions used to calculate employer withdrawal liability to be locked in by the end of the prior plan year or whether actuaries may select assumptions after that date. The outcome affects billions of dollars in potential obligations for thousands of employers in multiemployer pension plans.

positions.label: Assumptions must be fixed by measurement date
positions.summary: Holds that all actuarial assumptions must be determined as of the statutory measurement date (end of prior plan year), providing employers greater predictability about potential withdrawal liability.

circuits.name: Seventh Circuit
circuits.caseName: M & K Employee Solutions v. Trustees of the IAM National Pension Fund

positions.label: Assumptions may be selected after measurement date
positions.summary: Holds that the actuary may select actuarial assumptions after the statutory measurement date, allowing use of more current data even though the liability is measured as of year-end.

circuits.name: Second Circuit
circuits.caseName: Trustees of the IAM National Pension Fund v. M & K Employee Solutions

splits.relatedScotusTitle: M & K Employee Solutions v. Trustees of the IAM National Pension Fund

splits.legalQuestion: Whether the term of supervised release is tolled (paused) when a supervisee absconds from supervision, thereby extending the period during which the person can be found to have violated the conditions of supervised release.
splits.description: Circuits are split on whether federal courts may apply a judicially created 'fugitive tolling' doctrine to supervised release, effectively extending court-ordered conditions beyond the time specified in the judgment when a person absconds. This affects revocation hearings for thousands of individuals who abscond from supervised release each year.

positions.label: Supervised release is tolled during absconding
positions.summary: Holds that the term of supervised release is tolled while a supervisee is a fugitive, so the supervised release period does not run during absconding.

positions.label: Supervised release is not tolled
positions.summary: Holds that there is no statutory basis for tolling supervised release during absconding, and the term expires as stated in the judgment regardless of whether the supervisee absconds.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether Section 2244(b)(1)'s bar on relitigating previously raised claims in habeas proceedings applies to federal prisoners filing motions under Section 2255.
splits.description: Circuits are split 6-to-3 on whether the stricter gatekeeping requirement of § 2244(b)(1)—which categorically bars claims previously presented—applies to federal prisoners bringing successive § 2255 motions. If it applies, federal prisoners face a higher bar than Congress may have intended; if not, they need only meet § 2255(h)'s requirements of newly discovered evidence or a new constitutional rule.

positions.label: Section 2244(b)(1) applies to § 2255 motions
positions.summary: Holds that § 2244(b)(1)'s categorical bar on previously raised claims applies to federal prisoners filing successive § 2255 motions, imposing an additional gatekeeping requirement beyond § 2255(h).

positions.label: Section 2244(b)(1) does not apply to § 2255 motions
positions.summary: Holds that § 2244(b)(1) does not apply to federal prisoners' successive § 2255 motions, so they need only satisfy the requirements of § 2255(h).

splits.legalQuestion: Whether a candidate for federal office has Article III standing to challenge a state law that allows the counting of mail-in ballots received after Election Day.
splits.description: Circuits disagree on the standard for when political candidates have standing to bring pre-election challenges to voting procedures. The outcome determines whether candidates can challenge rules on mail-in voting and ballot counting deadlines before an election or must wait until after potential injury occurs.

positions.label: Candidates have standing
positions.summary: Holds that candidates have Article III standing to challenge election rules pre-election based on competitive injury from allegedly unlawful vote-counting procedures.

circuits.name: Seventh Circuit
circuits.caseName: Bost v. Illinois State Board of Elections

positions.label: Candidates lack standing
positions.summary: Holds that candidates do not have Article III standing to challenge election administration rules pre-election absent a concrete, particularized injury that is actual or imminent.

splits.relatedScotusTitle: Bost v. Illinois State Board of Elections

splits.legalQuestion: Whether the rule of lenity takes precedence over deference to agency interpretations (Auer/Kisor deference) when construing ambiguous provisions in the Sentencing Guidelines that carry criminal consequences.
splits.description: Circuits disagree on whether lenity trumps deference to the Sentencing Commission's commentary when interpreting ambiguous Guidelines provisions in criminal cases. The Third Circuit held that agency deference trumps lenity, while the Fourth, Fifth, and Eleventh Circuits hold that lenity prevails over deference. The issue has grown more significant after Loper Bright overruled Chevron.

positions.label: Lenity trumps deference
positions.summary: Holds that the rule of lenity, as an ancient canon of interpretation, takes precedence over agency or Sentencing Commission deference when construing ambiguous criminal provisions.

positions.label: Deference trumps lenity
positions.summary: Holds that when the Sentencing Commission's commentary reasonably interprets an ambiguous Guideline, the court defers to the Commission's interpretation rather than applying the rule of lenity in favor of the defendant.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether the word 'and' in the First Step Act's safety-valve provision, 18 U.S.C. § 3553(f)(1), is conjunctive (requiring all three criminal-history criteria to disqualify) or disjunctive (any one criterion disqualifies).
splits.description: Courts are deeply divided on whether 'and' in the safety-valve provision means a defendant must meet all three listed criminal-history criteria to be disqualified from safety-valve relief, or whether possessing any one criterion is disqualifying. The split results in dramatically different sentences for defendants with identical criminal histories depending on which circuit they are in.

positions.label: 'And' is conjunctive — all three criteria required to disqualify
positions.summary: Holds that a defendant is disqualified from safety-valve relief only if all three criminal-history criteria in § 3553(f)(1) are met, reading 'and' in its ordinary conjunctive sense.

positions.label: 'And' is disjunctive or distributive — any one criterion disqualifies
positions.summary: Holds that possessing any one of the three listed criminal-history criteria disqualifies a defendant from safety-valve relief, reading 'and' as disjunctive or distributive in context.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether the Sentencing Guidelines' definition of 'controlled substance offense' in § 4B1.2(b) includes inchoate offenses such as conspiracy, attempt, and aiding and abetting, based on Application Note 1's expansion of the guideline text.
splits.description: Circuits disagree on whether Application Note 1 to U.S.S.G. § 4B1.2, which adds inchoate offenses to the definition of 'controlled substance offense,' is a valid interpretation entitled to deference or impermissibly expands the guideline text. This determines whether conspiracy and attempt convictions can serve as career-offender predicates.

positions.label: Application Note 1 is valid and inchoate offenses qualify
positions.summary: Holds that Application Note 1 is an authoritative interpretation of § 4B1.2(b), and conspiracy and other inchoate offenses qualify as controlled substance offenses for career-offender purposes.

positions.label: Application Note 1 is invalid; inchoate offenses do not qualify
positions.summary: Holds that the guideline text unambiguously excludes inchoate offenses and Application Note 1 impermissibly expands the definition, so conspiracy and attempt do not qualify as career-offender predicates.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether non-retroactive changes in sentencing law (such as the First Step Act's anti-stacking amendment to § 924(c)) can be considered as a factor supporting 'extraordinary and compelling reasons' for compassionate release under 18 U.S.C. § 3582(c)(1)(A).
splits.description: Circuits are sharply divided on whether courts may consider non-retroactive changes in law—alone or combined with other factors—when evaluating compassionate release motions. The Eighth Circuit bars such consideration entirely, the Seventh Circuit also bars it, while other circuits have been more permissive. The Sentencing Commission's 2023 amendment to § 1B1.13(b)(6) attempted to resolve the issue, but courts disagree on whether the Commission had authority to do so.

positions.label: Non-retroactive changes cannot support compassionate release
positions.summary: Holds that a non-retroactive change in sentencing law, whether alone or in combination with other factors, cannot contribute to a finding of 'extraordinary and compelling reasons' for a sentence reduction under § 3582(c)(1)(A).

positions.label: Non-retroactive changes may be considered
positions.summary: Holds that courts may consider non-retroactive changes in sentencing law, particularly unusually long sentences resulting from stacked § 924(c) convictions, as part of the extraordinary and compelling reasons analysis for compassionate release.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether a state drug conviction qualifies as a 'controlled substance offense' under the Sentencing Guidelines when the state schedule of controlled substances is broader than the federal Controlled Substances Act.
splits.description: Circuits disagree on whether 'controlled substance offense' in U.S.S.G. § 4B1.2(b) requires the underlying substance to be federally controlled, or whether any state-law drug offense qualifies regardless of whether the substance is on the federal schedule. This affects career-offender and other sentencing enhancements for defendants with prior state drug convictions.

positions.label: State drug offenses qualify regardless of federal scheduling
positions.summary: Holds that the Guidelines broadly define 'controlled substance offense' to include any state-law drug offense, without requiring that the particular substance also be controlled under federal law.

positions.label: Substance must be federally controlled
positions.summary: Holds that a state drug conviction only qualifies as a 'controlled substance offense' under the Guidelines if the substance at issue is also controlled under federal law.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether post-petition, pre-conversion increases in the equity of an asset belong to the bankruptcy estate or to the debtor upon good-faith conversion from Chapter 13 to Chapter 7 under 11 U.S.C. § 348(f)(1)(A).
splits.description: Circuits disagree on whether appreciation in property value that occurs between the filing of a Chapter 13 petition and a good-faith conversion to Chapter 7 belongs to the bankruptcy estate or the debtor. The Ninth Circuit held that such appreciation belongs to the estate, while other circuits have held it belongs to the debtor, creating a split with significant consequences for debtors who convert in good faith.

positions.label: Post-petition appreciation belongs to the estate
positions.summary: Holds that under § 348(f)(1)(A), post-petition, pre-conversion increases in asset equity inure to the bankruptcy estate upon conversion from Chapter 13 to Chapter 7.

positions.label: Post-petition appreciation belongs to the debtor
positions.summary: Holds that § 348(f)(1)(A) protects good-faith converting debtors by limiting the estate to property as valued at the time of filing, so post-petition appreciation belongs to the debtor.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether the civil or criminal appellate deadline applies to appeals from orders on Hyde Amendment motions for attorney's fees in criminal cases.
splits.description: Circuits disagree on whether a Hyde Amendment motion for attorney's fees, though filed in a criminal case, is a civil matter subject to the 30-day civil appeal deadline or a criminal matter subject to the 14-day criminal appeal deadline. The Seventh Circuit joined the majority view that the civil deadline applies, but acknowledged this is a circuit split.

positions.label: Civil deadline applies
positions.summary: Holds that a Hyde Amendment motion for attorney's fees is a civil matter ancillary to the criminal case, so the 30-day civil appeal deadline under Fed. R. App. P. 4(a) applies.

positions.label: Criminal deadline applies
positions.summary: Holds that a Hyde Amendment motion is part of the criminal case and therefore the shorter 14-day criminal appeal deadline under Fed. R. App. P. 4(b) applies.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether federal prisoners challenging conditions of confinement or BOP decisions affecting the duration of custody must proceed under habeas corpus (28 U.S.C. § 2241) or may bring claims under civil rights statutes such as Bivens.
splits.description: For over fifty years, circuits have disagreed on the boundary between habeas corpus and civil rights claims for federal prisoners challenging BOP decisions that affect the terms of their confinement. This split affects whether prisoners must exhaust habeas remedies and which procedural framework governs their claims, including challenges to First Step Act time credit calculations.

positions.label: Habeas is the exclusive remedy for duration-of-custody challenges
positions.summary: Holds that when a prisoner challenges BOP decisions affecting the fact or duration of confinement, habeas corpus under § 2241 is the exclusive federal remedy.

positions.label: Conditions-of-confinement claims may proceed outside habeas
positions.summary: Holds that challenges to conditions of confinement, as opposed to the fact or duration of confinement, may proceed under civil rights statutes and are not limited to habeas corpus.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether district courts may impose a sentence upon revocation of supervised release that is intended to punish the defendant for the violation of supervised release conditions, or whether revocation sentences must be limited to addressing the breach of trust.
splits.description: Circuits disagree on whether the purpose of a revocation sentence is limited to sanctioning the breach of the court's trust inherent in violating supervised release conditions, or may also include punishment for the underlying violating conduct. The Sixth Circuit has been internally divided, with dissenters noting it is at the extreme of a circuit split allowing express punishment at revocation.

positions.label: Revocation sentence may include punishment
positions.summary: Holds that district courts may expressly punish defendants for violations of supervised release conditions at revocation, not just sanction the breach of trust.

positions.label: Revocation sentence limited to breach of trust
positions.summary: Holds that the purpose of revocation is to sanction the breach of the court's trust, and revocation sentences should not be used to punish for the underlying violation conduct.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether the Federal Aviation Administration Authorization Act of 1994 (FAAAA) preempts state-law negligence claims against freight brokers who arrange transportation of property by motor carriers.
splits.description: Circuits are split on whether the FAAAA's broad preemption clause, which prohibits states from enacting laws 'related to' a broker's services, bars state-law negligence claims when a freight broker selects a negligent motor carrier. This has significant implications for personal injury plaintiffs and the trucking industry.

positions.label: FAAAA preempts state negligence claims against brokers
positions.summary: Holds that state-law negligence claims against freight brokers are preempted because they relate to the broker's services in arranging transportation.

positions.label: FAAAA does not preempt state negligence claims against brokers
positions.summary: Holds that state-law negligence claims against freight brokers are not preempted by the FAAAA because they are generally applicable safety regulations, not regulations targeting broker services.

splits.relatedScotusTitle: Montgomery v. Caribe Transport II, LLC

splits.legalQuestion: Whether a district court may consider factors related to potential errors in a defendant's conviction or sentence as part of the 'extraordinary and compelling reasons' analysis for compassionate release under § 3582(c)(1)(A), or whether such claims must be raised exclusively through habeas corpus under § 2255.
splits.description: Circuits disagree on whether compassionate release motions can incorporate arguments about legal errors in the original conviction or sentence. Some circuits allow courts to consider such factors as part of the totality of circumstances, while others hold that these claims belong exclusively in habeas proceedings and cannot contribute to a compassionate release finding.

positions.label: Legal-error factors may be considered in compassionate release
positions.summary: Holds that district courts may consider potential legal errors in a conviction or sentence as one factor among others when evaluating whether extraordinary and compelling reasons support compassionate release.

positions.label: Legal-error claims must go through § 2255
positions.summary: Holds that claims related to errors in conviction or sentence are cognizable only through § 2255 habeas proceedings and may not be considered as extraordinary and compelling reasons for compassionate release.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether an order denying a motion to seal court filings containing private medical information is immediately appealable under the collateral order doctrine.
splits.description: Circuits have not uniformly addressed whether denial of a motion to seal medical records qualifies for immediate appeal under the collateral order doctrine. The D.C. Circuit held such orders are immediately appealable as a value of 'high order' effectively unreviewable after final judgment, which may create tension with circuits that have been more restrictive about collateral order jurisdiction.

positions.label: Immediately appealable under collateral order doctrine
positions.summary: Holds that an order denying sealing of medical records is immediately appealable because maintaining privacy in medical treatments is a value of high order that would be effectively unreviewable after final judgment.

positions.label: Not immediately appealable
positions.summary: Holds that orders denying sealing motions generally do not satisfy the collateral order doctrine's requirements because the interest can be adequately protected by other means such as protective orders or post-judgment appeal.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether 18 U.S.C. § 922(g)(3), which prohibits unlawful users of controlled substances from possessing firearms, violates the Second Amendment as applied to marijuana users.
splits.description: Following the Supreme Court's decision in Bruen, circuits have split on whether the longstanding prohibition on firearm possession by users of controlled substances survives Second Amendment scrutiny when applied to marijuana users. Some circuits have upheld the statute while others have found it unconstitutional as applied.

positions.label: Section 922(g)(3) is constitutional
positions.summary: Holds that § 922(g)(3) is consistent with the historical tradition of firearm regulation and does not violate the Second Amendment as applied to marijuana users.

positions.label: Section 922(g)(3) is unconstitutional as applied
positions.summary: Holds that § 922(g)(3) violates the Second Amendment as applied to marijuana users because the government has not demonstrated a sufficient historical tradition of disarming persons who use intoxicating substances.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether workers who distribute baked goods to retail stores qualify as 'transportation workers engaged in foreign or interstate commerce' under Section 1 of the Federal Arbitration Act, thereby exempting them from mandatory arbitration agreements.
splits.description: Circuits disagree on how broadly to read the Section 1 exemption for transportation workers, particularly for last-mile delivery workers who transport goods within a single state as part of an interstate chain of commerce. This affects whether such workers can be compelled to arbitrate employment disputes.

positions.label: Broad reading — last-mile workers exempt
positions.summary: Holds that workers who are the last link in an interstate chain of commerce, even if operating within a single state, qualify as transportation workers engaged in interstate commerce and are exempt from the FAA.

circuits.name: First Circuit
circuits.caseName: Fraga v. Premium Retail Services, Inc.

positions.label: Narrow reading — last-mile workers not exempt
positions.summary: Holds that workers who deliver goods locally within a state do not qualify as transportation workers engaged in interstate commerce merely because the goods previously moved in interstate commerce.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether Section 47(b) of the Investment Company Act creates a private right of action allowing parties to a contract that violates the ICA to sue in federal court for rescission of that contract.
splits.description: Circuits disagree on whether § 47(b) of the ICA provides an implied private right of action for rescission of contracts made in violation of the Act. This affects the ability of private parties to enforce the ICA's protections against abusive investment company practices through direct federal court actions.

positions.label: Section 47(b) creates a private right of action
positions.summary: Holds that § 47(b) creates an implied private right of action for rescission of contracts that violate the Investment Company Act, allowing private parties to sue directly.

circuits.name: Second Circuit
circuits.caseName: FS Credit Opportunities Corp. v. Saba Capital Master Fund

positions.label: Section 47(b) does not create a private right of action
positions.summary: Holds that § 47(b) is a voidability provision rather than a private right of action, and only the SEC can enforce the ICA's prohibitions.

splits.relatedScotusTitle: FS Credit Opportunities Corp. v. Saba Capital Master Fund

splits.legalQuestion: Whether the Federal Insecticide, Fungicide, and Rodenticide Act (FIFRA) preempts state-law failure-to-warn claims based on allegedly inadequate product labeling when the EPA has not required the specific warning.
splits.description: Circuits are split on whether FIFRA's labeling requirements preempt state tort claims alleging that a pesticide manufacturer should have included additional warnings on its label. This affects products liability litigation involving herbicides and pesticides nationwide, including high-profile Roundup litigation.

positions.label: FIFRA preempts state failure-to-warn claims
positions.summary: Holds that FIFRA preempts state-law failure-to-warn claims because allowing such claims would impose labeling requirements 'in addition to or different from' those required under FIFRA.

positions.label: FIFRA does not preempt state failure-to-warn claims
positions.summary: Holds that state failure-to-warn claims are not preempted by FIFRA because they parallel, rather than impose requirements 'in addition to or different from,' federal labeling requirements.

splits.relatedScotusTitle: Monsanto Company v. John L. Durnell

splits.legalQuestion: Whether Section 2 of the Voting Rights Act, as construed under the Gingles framework, can constitutionally compel race-based redistricting as a remedy for vote dilution, and whether compliance with Section 2 constitutes a compelling governmental interest justifying the use of race in drawing district lines.
splits.description: This case raises the question of whether race-conscious redistricting mandated by Section 2 of the Voting Rights Act is constitutionally permissible under the Equal Protection Clause. Lower courts have applied the Gingles framework to require consideration of race, but the constitutionality of this framework as applied is increasingly contested.

positions.label: Section 2 compels constitutionally permissible race-conscious redistricting
positions.summary: Holds that compliance with Section 2 of the VRA, as interpreted under the Gingles framework, constitutes a compelling interest that justifies the use of race in drawing district lines.

positions.label: Section 2's compelled race-based redistricting raises constitutional concerns
positions.summary: Holds or suggests that the Gingles framework's requirement of race-based redistricting may exceed constitutional limits under the Equal Protection Clause, particularly after Dobbs-era equal protection jurisprudence.

circuits.name: Eighth Circuit
circuits.caseName: Arkansas State Conference of the NAACP v. Arkansas Board of Apportionment

splits.legalQuestion: What legal standards govern claims of race discrimination under 42 U.S.C. § 1981 in the operation of hospitality or business establishments, particularly regarding the scope of the right to make and enforce contracts?
splits.description: Circuits have taken different approaches to the standard for § 1981 claims in hospitality and service contexts, particularly regarding what constitutes interference with the right to make and enforce contracts and the available remedies. The Supreme Court granted certiorari to resolve the Second Circuit's approach against other circuits' frameworks.

positions.label: Narrower standard for § 1981 claims
positions.summary: Holds that § 1981 claims require a showing of interference with an actual contractual relationship or attempted contract, applying a more restrictive view of what constitutes actionable discrimination.

positions.label: Broader standard for § 1981 claims
positions.summary: Holds that § 1981 covers a broader range of discriminatory conduct in business establishments, including degraded service or hostile treatment that impairs the enjoyment of contracted services.
